title: GLP-1s, Fat Loss & Food Freedom: Your Body Isn't Broken
guestBio: Rebecca Maas is a holistic clinician, speaker, and educator with credentials as a Holistic Health Practitioner, Certified Clinical Master Herbalist, and Certified Nutritionist. Founder of The Maas Method, she has spent over 15 years helping people optimize metabolic health using food as medicine, blending systems biology with functional nutrition and narrative medicine. She works with clients internationally via telehealth and is the author of the upcoming book The Holistic Guide to Thriving on GLP-1s.
excerpt: If you have ever felt like your body is working against you, this conversation is going to change everything. Holistic clinician Rebecca Maas joins Aimee to reframe GLP-1 medications, metabolic health, and fat loss in a way that is rooted in science, steeped in compassion, and completely free of diet culture shame. This one is for every woman who is done punishing her body and ready to partner with it instead.
metaDescription: Holistic clinician Rebecca Maas joins Aimee to unpack GLP-1s, fat loss, and metabolic health for women — without shame or diet culture noise.
keyTakeaways: Your symptoms are not inconveniences — they are signals your body is sending you, and learning to listen to them is the foundation of real metabolic health.
GLP-1 medications are a tool, not a miracle drug, and they only work as part of a whole-body strategy that includes real nourishment, muscle preservation, and provider-led care.
Fat loss and weight loss are not the same thing — protecting your muscle mass is essential to long-term metabolic strength and should be the true goal.
Losing weight too quickly can backfire by releasing stored toxins faster than your body can process them, which is why a slow and intentional approach matters.
Obesogens — chemicals found in plastics, processed foods, and everyday products — can flip a biological 'fat switch' and disrupt your metabolism in ways that have nothing to do with willpower.
Awareness, not just discipline, is what sustains lasting health: once you understand how food affects your mood, energy, and body, the choices become intuitive.
Mothers are the CEOs of household health, and the knowledge you build about your own body is a legacy you pass directly to your children.

pullQuotes.text: Your symptoms are signals — an invitation, not an inconvenience.
We don't want weight loss. We want fat loss and a stronger body.
If you want the thing, eat the thing — then notice how you feel.
Health is a partnership with the body, never a war against it.
Discipline gets you there. Awareness keeps you there.

quiz.title: What Is Your Relationship With Your Body Telling You?
quiz.description: Answer these six questions to discover how you currently relate to your health — and what shift might unlock everything. There are no wrong answers here, only honest ones.

A.name: The Intuitive Investigator
A.description: You have always sensed that your body is trying to tell you something — you just have not always had the language or the permission to listen. You are naturally curious about the connection between how you feel and how you live, and you tend to ask 'why' before you accept 'just because.' You may have already experimented with nutrition, wellness tools, or functional approaches, and you are drawn to the idea that your story is clinically useful. This episode is going to feel like a conversation you have been waiting to have — Rebecca Maas will give you a framework that finally matches the way your mind already works.

B.name: The Determined Doer
B.description: You are not afraid of hard work — in fact, hard work is kind of your love language. When you decide to do something, you do it fully, and you bring that same intensity to your health goals. The challenge is that discipline alone has taken you as far as it can, and you may be starting to feel the ceiling. You are ready for the next layer: awareness, strategy, and a more nuanced understanding of what your body actually needs versus what you have been pushing it to do. This episode will give you the science and the permission to work smarter, not just harder.

C.name: The Quiet Caretaker
C.description: You have spent so long taking care of everyone else that your own health has quietly moved to the bottom of the list — not because you do not care, but because you are genuinely stretched thin and the invisible labor is real. You show up for your family, your team, and your community with everything you have, and sometimes there is simply nothing left for you. You may have normalized fatigue, hormonal shifts, or a body that feels unfamiliar as just part of the deal. This episode is a reminder that your health is not a luxury — it is the foundation everything else is built on, and you deserve to prioritize it without guilt.

D.name: The Skeptical Strategist
D.description: You have seen enough wellness trends come and go that your default is to raise an eyebrow first and ask questions before you buy in. You are not cynical — you are discerning, and that is actually a strength. You want evidence, nuance, and honesty, not hype. The GLP-1 conversation has probably exhausted you because almost nobody is telling the full story. This episode is exactly what you have been looking for: a clinician who leads with science, acknowledges complexity, and refuses to oversimplify. Rebecca Maas will earn your trust — and give you genuinely useful tools in return.

questions.question: When something feels off with your body — fatigue, bloating, mood shifts — what is your first instinct?

options.text: I start connecting the dots — sleep, food, stress — trying to figure out what changed.
I push through and hope it resolves. I do not have time to slow down.
I add it to the list of things I will deal with eventually, once everyone else is settled.
I research it thoroughly before I decide whether it is worth addressing.

questions.question: A wellness tool you have been curious about is getting a lot of buzz — think GLP-1s, continuous glucose monitors, peptides. What do you do?

options.text: I dig into the mechanism — I want to understand how it works before I decide if it is right for me.
If it produces results, I am willing to try it. I can optimize later.
I probably do not get around to exploring it — I am managing enough already.
I wait for the noise to die down and look for the peer-reviewed evidence.

questions.question: How do you typically talk to yourself when your health goals feel out of reach?

options.text: I try to get curious instead of critical — there is usually a reason I am struggling.
I get frustrated with myself and double down with more discipline.
I tell myself I will focus on it when things calm down, even though things rarely calm down.
I question whether my strategy is correct and look for a better approach.

questions.question: When you think about your relationship with food, which description feels most honest?

options.text: I am learning to see food as information — I notice how different things make me feel.
Food is fuel. I eat to perform, and I am pretty strict about it.
I feed everyone else first and eat whatever is left, whenever I can.
I am skeptical of most nutrition advice because so much of it contradicts itself.

questions.question: Someone close to you mentions they are considering a GLP-1 medication. How do you respond?

options.text: I ask them about their overall plan — medication alone does not seem like the whole picture.
I respect the decision — if it gets results, it is worth exploring.
I support them fully while quietly wondering if I should be looking at my own health more seriously.
I ask a lot of questions about their provider, the protocol, and the evidence behind it.

questions.question: What does your ideal relationship with your health actually look like?

options.text: A genuine partnership — I want to understand my body and work with it, not against it.
Consistent, measurable progress — I want to hit my goals and feel in control.
Simple, sustainable, and not one more thing I have to manage perfectly.
Evidence-based and personalized — not generic advice, but a real strategy built for me.

content:
Let's start with something you may have never heard from a clinician, a wellness influencer, or a diet program: your body is not broken. It is not betraying you. It is not working against you. It is doing exactly what it was designed to do — responding intelligently to the environment you are living in, the food you are eating, the stress you are carrying, and the signals it has been given. The problem is not your body. The problem is that nobody taught you how to listen to it.

That is exactly where this conversation begins. In Episode 67 of The Manage Her® Podcast, Aimee Rickabus sits down with Rebecca Maas — holistic clinician, Certified Clinical Master Herbalist, Certified Nutritionist, and founder of The Maas Method — for one of the most grounded, shame-free, and genuinely useful conversations about women's metabolic health you will find anywhere. If you have been confused about GLP-1 medications, frustrated by the scale, or quietly exhausted by decades of diet culture noise, this episode is the reset you did not know you needed.

Your Symptoms Are Speaking. Are You Listening?

Rebecca Maas was raised in a household where food was medicine long before that phrase became a marketing slogan. Herbs, homeopathy, and whole ingredients were simply how her family operated. That foundation shaped the clinician she became — one who blends systems biology with functional nutrition and something called narrative medicine, the clinical philosophy that your story belongs in the exam room. Your history, your habits, your stress load, your relationships — all of it is data. All of it is relevant.

Too often, women are handed a diagnosis, a prescription, or a meal plan without anyone asking the deeper question: What is your body trying to tell you? Rebecca's approach flips the script. Symptoms are not inconveniences to suppress — they are signals, invitations to look closer. A body that is struggling is not a body that has failed. It is a body that is communicating. Learning that language changes everything about how you relate to your own health.

The GLP-1 Conversation We Actually Need

GLP-1 medications are everywhere right now — in headlines, in Hollywood, in your group chat. And with that ubiquity comes a lot of noise: miracle drug hype on one side, moral panic on the other. Rebecca cuts through both with rare clarity. GLP-1s are a tool. A legitimate, clinically useful tool — but a tool that only works when it is held correctly, which means with real food, muscle preservation, adequate protein, and a provider who actually knows what they are doing.

One of the most important distinctions Rebecca makes is the difference between weight loss and fat loss. These are not the same thing, and conflating them is where so many women go wrong. Rapid weight loss — the kind a GLP-1 can produce when used without proper support — often means losing muscle alongside fat. And losing muscle is metabolically devastating. It slows your resting metabolism, compromises your strength, and sets you up for the exact rebound cycle you were trying to escape. The goal is never a smaller number on the scale. The goal is a leaner, stronger, more metabolically resilient body.

She also introduces a concept that will stick with you: the snow globe metaphor for detox. When you lose fat quickly, you are not just shrinking cells — you are releasing the toxins that were stored inside them. If your detox pathways, particularly your glutathione system, are not supported and functioning well, those toxins re-circulate. Your body gets overwhelmed. And the fallout can look like fatigue, brain fog, inflammation, or a plateau that feels completely inexplicable. Going low and slow is not weakness. It is wisdom.

The Environment Your Biology Did Not Evolve For

Here is something that might genuinely reframe the way you think about your body and your choices: obesogens. These are chemicals — found in plastics, pesticides, processed food packaging, and countless everyday products — that can biologically trigger what Rebecca calls a fat switch. BPA is one of the most well-known culprits, but it is far from alone. These compounds interact with your hormones and your epigenetics in ways that your grandparents' bodies simply did not have to contend with.

This is not about fear. It is about context. When you understand that the modern food supply and environment have changed the biological landscape your body is navigating, you can stop blaming yourself for struggles that were never entirely within your control. Knowledge is not an excuse — it is a starting point. And for women who have spent years in a shame spiral around food, weight, and willpower, that starting point is everything.

The Pantry as a Power Move

Practical change does not require a complete overhaul overnight. Rebecca's approach to pantry makeovers is refreshingly simple: apply a rubric. Ask of everything on your shelf — is this actually food? Can you trace its ingredients back to something that grew, walked, swam, or was harvested? If the answer requires a chemistry degree, that is useful information. You do not have to throw everything out on day one. You just have to start seeing what you are working with.

For mothers especially — and Aimee and Rebecca both speak to this directly — the pantry is not just personal. It is the first classroom your children have about nutrition, pleasure, energy, and their relationship to food. The way you talk about ingredients, the way you respond when your child wants a treat, the way you model curiosity instead of rigidity — all of it shapes the next generation's metabolic and emotional relationship with eating. You are not just feeding your family. You are educating them.

Partnership, Not Punishment

Perhaps the most powerful thread running through this entire conversation is the idea of partnership. Not discipline alone — though discipline matters. Not restriction — though discernment has its place. But a genuine, ongoing, curious relationship with your body in which you are paying attention, responding to feedback, and making choices from a place of self-respect rather than self-punishment.

Aimee shares her own story in this episode — navigating PCOS, metabolic syndrome, and the particular challenge of being a high-performing woman who was doing everything right on paper and still feeling like her body was a problem to manage. Her vulnerability here is a gift, because it names something so many women in leadership experience: the disconnect between outer achievement and inner physical struggle. And Rebecca's response is the one every woman deserves to hear. Your body was never the enemy. It was always waiting for a partner.

If you have ever felt exhausted by the war — with food, with your weight, with your reflection — this episode gives you permission to lay down your weapons. Not because the work is over, but because the work changes entirely when you stop fighting and start listening. That is where the real transformation lives. And it is available to you right now.

This conversation is educational and is not medical advice. GLP-1 medications, peptides, and supplements should only be used under the care of a qualified, licensed provider.
